20 Sep 1942 At 0018 A/20, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) left patrol to return to Lerwick.
At 1908 A/20, HMS Tigris changed course to proceed to patrol in zones K 153 and K 154 (near Andenes, Norway to the north of Harstad) as ordered in FO(S)'s signal timed 1800 A/20. (5)
27 Sep 1942 At 2037 A/27, on surfacing after a day's submerged patrol, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) left patrol to return to Lerwick as ordered in FO(S)'s signal timed 1926 A/26. (5)

1 Oct 1942 Around 1905 A/1, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) ended her 15th war patrol at Lerwick. She departed for Methil around 2015 A/1, escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (5)
3 Oct 1942 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) arrived at Methil. (25)
4 Oct 1942 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) departed Methil for Sheerness. She made the pasage in convoy FS 25. (37)
6 Oct 1942 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) arrived at Sheerness. She then proceeded to Chatham Dockyard for repairs and the fitting of RDF (radar). (25)
6 Nov 1942 With her repairs completed, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) departed from Sheerness for Portsmouth. (37)

7 Nov 1942 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) arrived at Portsmouth. She departed for Gibraltar around 1345 A/7. En-route she is diverted for a short patrol in the Bay of Biscay to intercept a German blockade breaker but she sighted nothing. (37)
15 Nov 1942 Around 1300 A/15, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. (25)

25 Nov 1942 Around 1600 A/25, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) departed from Gibraltar for her 16th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She is to perform an anti-U-boat patrol to the south-west of Sardinia. (5)
29 Nov 1942 Around 2300 A/29, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) arrived in position Z (38°01'N, 07°30'E). She then commenced to patrol within 20 nautical miles from this position. (5)
5 Dec 1942 At 0055 A/5, in position 37°56'N, 07°37'E, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) picked up HE at 220 revs. bearing 110°.
At 0057 A/5, the HE was crossing to the south-west. All torpedo tubes were made ready and course was altered to close.
At 0104 A/5, what are thought to be three destroyers were seen to pass ahead at an estimated range of 5 nautical miles. Their course was about 230° at about 22 knots.
At 0118 A/5, HE faded out on bearing 230°.

Most likely this was 'Force Q' which conducted an anti-shipping raid during the night of 4/5 December 1942. Force Q at that time was made up of the light cruisers HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, CB, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral C.H.J. Harcourt, CBE, RN), HMS Argonaut (Capt. E.W.L. Longley-Cook, RN) and the destroyers HMS Eskimo (Capt. J.W.M. Eaton, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Tartar (Cdr. St.J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN).
At 1638 A/5, HMS Tigris received Capt. S 8's signal timed 0940 A/5, ordering her to shift patrol to the Naples area. Course was altered to comply. (5)

6 Dec 1942 The Italian submarine Porfido was torpedoed and sunk about 90 nautical miles north-north-east off Bone (Annaba), Algeria in position 38°10'N, 08°35'E by the British submarine HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN).

0143 A/6 - In position 38°10'N, 08°35'E the Officer of the Watch (Lt. R.G. Sampson, RN) sighted a vessel broad on the Port bow. This he instantly and correctly judged to be a submarine end-on. Lieutenant Sampson immediately swung Tigris towards under full helm(she was doing 12.5 knots), gave the alarm and reduced speed. At the moment Lt.Cdr. Colvin arrived on the bridge the two submarines were practically head on, range about 1200 yards. It was obvious that the other vessel was a submarine steering towards. As P 219 was known to be returning from patrol and might very well be the submarine encountered an immediate attack was out of the question.

0145 A/6 - Stopped both engines and when about to make the challenge when the other submarine altered course to port. Range was 600 yards and Lt.Cdr. Colvin was now able to identify the submarine as enemy. She closely resembled a German type 7 U-boat.

0146 A/6 - Fired two torpedoes from 600 yards. The first torpedo ran under the stern of the enemy the second torpedo hit between the conning tower and the stern. Tigris closed the scene and rescued one Italian officer and three ratings. (5)
8 Dec 1942 Around 1350 A/8, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) arrived in her patrol area near Naples. (5)

9 Dec 1942 At 1415 A/9, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN), which was patrolling near Ischia Island, picked up HE and saw a motor A/S boat appear over the horizon coming out of the direction of Ventotene Island. The boat crossed to the south of HMS Tigris but then turned straight towards. HMS Tigris then went to 120 feet. The MASB , which up to then had been doing around 15 knots stopped on the submarines starboard beam and remained there for about 15 minutes. It then passed directly overhead and then stopped on the port beam.

HMS Tigris then lost depth control and took a bow down angle. The dive could only be checked at 300 feet but after 3 minutes started to sink deeper to 350 feet. This all made quite a bit of noise and the enemy surely must have picked it up. There was no other choice then to blow ballast and after some time a stable trim was caught and held at 90 feet. The enemy dropped three depth charges but these were only small and were also dropped astern.
[We have been unable to identify this 'MAS(B)-boat'.]

At 1847 A/9, HMS Tigris received Capt. S 10's signal timed 1240 A/9 ordering submarines on patrol near Naples to new positions. HMS Tigris was to patrol in position 258° - Punta Carena (Capri Island) - 10 nautical miles until 1100 B/9. Course was set accordingly. (5)

10 Dec 1942 At 0750 A/10, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) sighted two masts about 8 nautical miles to the eastward. A ship was rounding Carena Point bound north. HE was picked up at 180 revs. It appeared to be a small ship and may even have been an auxiliary schooner.
At 1205 A/10, HMS Tigris, received Capt. S 10's signal timed 1051 A/10, ordering her to patrol in position 300°- Carena Point - 14 nautical miles. Course was set accordingly.

At 1415 A/10, an auxiliary A/S schooner was sighted to the north t a range of 4 nautical miles. She was steering west. Course was altered to pass astern of the schooner. Later HMS Tigris altered course to the west to avoid ending up in one of the other submarines billet. Range to the A/S schooner was kept at a minimum of 4 nautical miles.
At 1752 A/10, when about to surface, the A/S schooner, which had been laying stopped to listen, was heard to start it engine(s).
At 1800 A/10, the schooner was seen to close HMS Tigris on bearing 035°.
At 1810 A/10, the bearing of the schooners HE was steady on 35°. Revs. were counted at 220. HMS Tigris turned away to port at slow speed and went to 70 feet.
At 1821 A/10, the schooner was very close. Revs. were now counted as 130.
At 1843 A/10, HE faded out on bearing 230°. HMS Tigris subsequently surfaced at 1859 A/10.

At 1920 A/10, Turbine HE was picked up at 200 revs. bearing 045°. On this bearing the land background of Ischia Island made it impossible to see clearly whereas Tigris was silhouetted against the light horizon with a setting moon. Tigris therefore dived. HE was not heard again but Tigris remained dived to allow the moon to set.

At 2040 A/10, Tigris surfaced in position 232° - Imperatore Lighthouse - 6.5 nautical miles. Course was then set to position 260° - Imperatore Lighthouse - 7 nautical miles where it was hoped to be able to charge undisturbed and where the point of arrival of inward bound shipping as mentioned in Capt. S 8's signal timed 1258 A/5 could also be covered.
[We have been unable to identify these small vessels.] (5)
11 Dec 1942 At 0137 A/10, diesel HE at 180 revs. was picked up by HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN). This was thought to be an A/S schooner passing to the southward.
At 0200 A/10, HMS Tigris completed charging. Course was then set on 090° to proceed to the patrol position.
At 0357 A/10, a darkened vessel was seen to the south steering to the north-west. HMS Tigris turned towards to close.
At 0405 A/10, the vessel was seen to be an A/S schooner. HMS Tigris altered away and resumed her original course.
At 0408 A/10, faint HE was picked up on bearing 070° but nothing was visible against the land background of Ischia Island. HMS Tigris altered course to 025°.
At 0438 A/10, definite HE was picked up bearing 075°. Diesel at 140 revs.

At 0441 A/10, HE was increasing on the same bearing. HMS Tigris dived in position 225° - Imperatore Lighthouse - 4 nautical miles and altered course to 320°. HE continued to be heard on the same bearing until around 0530 A/10. The vessel appeared to be circling. Perhaps it was HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN).
0630 A/10, HMS Tigris altered course to 180° to close the patrol position.

At 0645 A/10, two A/S schooners were seen to the southward. HMS Tigris therefore retired to the northwards. These two schooners patrolled to the south and east of Imperatore Lighthouse throughout the day. HMS Tigris therefore patrolled to the north of the route between Ischia and Ventotene Island.
At 1818 A/10, HMS Tigris surfaced in position 335° - Imperatore Lighthouse - 12.5 nautical miles. Nothing further had been seen all day.

12 Dec 1942 At 0510 A/12, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) dived in position 337° - Imperatore Lighthouse - 11 nautical miles. She then set course to the south, skirting the west coast of Ischia Island about 4 to 5 nautical miles from the shore to proceed to the patrol position. Again two A/S schooners were seen on the line of advance. HMS Tigris then commenced to work round them to the west and the south.
At 1900 A/12, HMS Tigris surfaced in position 337° - Imperatore Lighthouse - 13 nautical miles. Course was then set to the south-west to charge.
At 2200 A/12, HMS Tigris set course for Algiers as ordered in Capt. S 10's signal timed 1950 A/11.

16 Dec 1942 Around 1140 A/16, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. She was escorted in by HMS Stornoway (T/A/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Fraser, RNR) which had joined at 0925 A/16. (5)
27 Dec 1942 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) departed from Algiers for Malta. (25)
2 Jan 1943 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta. At Malta HMS Tigris is docked before she proceeded on her next patrol. [Dates not known to us.] (25)

14 Jan 1943 Around 1300 A/14, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) departed from Malta for her 17th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the West coast of Greece and the lower Adriatic.
[No log is available so no map can be displayed.] (5)

21 Jan 1943 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant Citta di Genova (5413 GRT, built 1930) in the Strait of Otranto about 25 nautical miles west of Saseno Island in position 40°32'N, 18°45'E.

The Citta di Genova, which was en-route from Patras to Bari, was carrying 200 military personnel and 158 Greek PoWs. 173 men are killed or missing, the survivors are picked up by the auxiliary Eolo and a fishing vessel.

1030 A/21, Sighted a 3-masted schooner of about 500 tons proceeding on course 110° at 7 knots. It was a tempting gunnery target but Lt.Cdr. Colvin decided not to do so in the hope something bigger would show up. A dummy attack with the stern tube was then made on her.
1250 A/21 - Sighted a medium seized merchant vessel bearing 116°. Distance was about 5 nautical miles. Started attack.

1313 A/21 - In position in position 40°32'N, 18°45'E fired four torpedoes from 1200 yards. One torpedo was seen to hit the ship under the funnel 63 seconds after firing the first torpedo. The ship swung 90° to Starboard and lost way. Boats were lowered very promptly and the target was seen to settle down slightly on an even keel with no list.
1315 A/21 - Tigris turned to port to retire.
1320 A/21 - The target appeared not to be settling any deeper so decided to finish her off with another torpedo.

1325 A/21 - Fired the stern tube. The target was hit 78 seconds later and she immediately dipped sharply by the bows. The whole after part lifted clear of the water. She went down in a vertical dive 90 seconds later. (5)

23 Jan 1943 At 1030 A/23, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) which was patrolling near Bar, sighted what was thought to be an ex Yugoslav torpedo boat approaching from the north-west at about 12 knots. She stopped several times for about 20 minutes dropping listening charges.
This was was most probably the T 1 (ex-Yugoslav) who sailed from Teodo at 0710 B/23 and arrived at Durazzo at 1520 B/23.
At 1045 A/23, the torpedo boat dropped a listening charge about 2 miles to the north-west and then went past about a mile to the seaward of HMS Tigris.
At 1120 A/23, the torpedo boat was lost from sight to the south-east.
At 1140 A/23, a small northbound coaster, in ballast and of about 600 tons, was sighted passing to the inshore of HMS Tigris.
At 1230 A/23, a small northbound coaster, in ballast and of about 900 tons, was sighted passing to the inshore of HMS Tigris.
Both coasters were suitable gun targets but Lt.Cdr. Colvin decided not to engage with the gun in the hope something bigger would show up. (5)

24 Jan 1943 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) fires four torpedoes against the Italian water tanker Cassala (1771 GRT, built 1902) near Ulcinj, Montenegro, Yugoslavia in position 41°55'N, 19°08'E. All torpedoes missed their target.

Cassala was escorted by the Italian (former Yugoslav) torpedo boat T 1 and proceeding from Durazzo to Teodo. The torpedo boat combed the tracks and dropped all her sixteen depth charges in three runs, claiming the submarine sunk.
1057 A/24 - Sighted a merchant vessel of about 2500 tons, escorted by a Partenope class destroyer approaching from the South-East. Range was about 6000 yards. Started attack.

1113 A/24 - In position 41°55'N, 19°08'E fired four torpedoes from 3000 yards. All missed as it appeared the targets speed was overestimated. Tigris went to 80 feet after firing. Tigris was hunted by the destroyer for about half an hour in which 14 depth charges were dropped single or in pairs.
1330 A/24 - Came to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. Went deep again and retired to seaward. (5)
27 Jan 1943 Around 1220 A/27, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) received Capt. S 10's signal timed 1051 A/27 ordering her to patrol in the Cape Dukato area on the west coast of Greece.
On surfacing at 2000 A/27, course was set to comply with these orders. (5)

28 Jan 1943 At 0930 A/28, when in position 40°32'N, 18°46'E, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) sighted a convoy bearing 190° at a range of about 10 nautical miles. The convoy was proceeding on an estimated course of 290° and was made up of merchant vessel of about 4000 tons and four smaller vessels of which only some masts and smoke could be seen. No air escort was seen.

This convoy was most probably the one made up of the transports Ascianghi (Italian, 610 GRT, built 1921), Apuania (Italian, 275 GRT, built 1917), Vesta (Italian 3351 GRT, built 1923) and Raffio (Italian, 316 GRT, built 1912) escorted by the auxiliary Rovigno. They sailed from Valona at 0500 B/28 and arrived at Brindisi at 1400 B/28. (5)

30 Jan 1943 At 1200 A/30, while patrolling in the Cape Dukato area, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) sighted the mast of a small vessel leaving Denata Bay steering northwards close inshore. Nothing further was seen for the remainder of the day.
On surfacing HMS Tigris set course for the Straits of Otranto. (5)

31 Jan 1943 At 0445 A/31, in position 40°20'N, 18°57'E, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) sighted a small vessel fairly close on the port bow, bearing 330°. Visibility was poor. HMS Tigris dived. HE was heard to cross ahead, 60 revs, reciprocating. It faded out on bearing 070°. It was at first thought to have been a patrol vessel but more likely it was a small ship bound for Corfu.

At 1250 A/31, HMS Tigris sighted smoke bearing 320°. This turned out to be three small trawler-type vessels, apparently in line abreast, steering a course of 115° along the Brindisi - Corfu route at not more then 5 knots. Avoiding action was taken by HMS Tigris which remained at periscope depth. The vessels passed three nautical miles to the northward at 1430 A/31.
On surfacing at 1840 A/31, HMS Tigris set course to return to the Cape Dukato area.
[We have been unable to identify the small vessels sighted on this day.] (5)
1 Feb 1943 On surfacing at 1810 A/1, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) left the Cape Dukato area to return to Malta. (5)
4 Feb 1943 Around 1130 A/4, HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). (5)
18 Feb 1943 HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, DSC, RN) departed from Malta for her 18th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Tyrrhenian Sea off Naples, Italy.
[No log is available so no map can be displayed.] (41)

22 Feb 1943 At 2330 B/22, the Italian merchant vessel, Teramo (1599 GRT, built 1920, former French Marie-Therese le Borgne) reports being attacked by a submarine with gunfire and torpedoes in 41°21'N, 12°53'E (near Cape Circeo). The torpedo boat Cigno sails from Naples to her assistance. If this attack was genuine there is a remote possibility that the attacker was HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) but the place of the attack is well to the north of her patrol area making this unlikely.

27 Feb 1943 The auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2201 (Flotilla leader) is towing to Naples UJ 2209, which had been damaged by air attack, in company with UJ 2210 when, at 1047 B/27 February, in Quadrat CJ 6782 (south of Capri, approximately 40°27'N, 014°18'E), UJ 2201 detects a submarine echo at over 3000 meters. UJ 2210 (Leutnant zur See der Reserve Otto Pollmann) is ordered to investigate. At 1053 B/27, the submarine chaser obtains a good echo at a distance of 2400 meters bearing 110° and turns sharply to starboard. The echo is clear at it appears the submarine is trying to take evasive action at 6 knots. Contact is lost at a distance of 180 meters but at 1114 B/27, UJ 2210 drops a first pattern of five depth charges set at 70, 70, 90, 120 and 120 meters. More depth charges will follow and contact will be renewed and finally after a last pattern of 15 depth charges dropped at 1234 B/27, an oil slick is observed. The hunt is finally ended at 1530 B/27 when contact was lost. It is believed that this attack accounted for HMS Tigris.
Six officers and fifty-six ratings are missing after the submarine failed to return from patrol.
